generations.text:
My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now not with the hope of rekindling the fire that once warmed our home, but with the urgency of a man who has seen the sea claim many a soul and knows that time is a cruel and fickle thing. The wind howls like a beast in the night, and the waves, they rise with a fury that makes me tremble. I have no doubt that the storm will test the very strength of this lighthouse, and I fear I may not be the one to see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left, and though I have kept the light burning for all who pass, I have not found the strength to keep the light of our love alive. I have tried, Eleanor, I have tried. I have written, I have waited, I have prayed that you would return, but you have not. And so I have grown used to silence, and I have come to accept that the path we once walked together has long since turned into a shadow.

Yet tonight, as I stand upon the cliffs, the salt air stinging my eyes and the sea roaring in my ears, I am reminded of the nights we once shared, when the firelight danced upon the walls and your laughter echoed through the halls. I recall the way you would sit by the window, watching the waves, and how you would say, “The sea is a mirror, Father, and it reflects the soul of the man who gazes

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee through the cracks of the lighthouse, the sea roiling like a beast in pain. The storm is coming—fierce, unrelenting, and I fear it may take more than the lamp and the tower. I’ve stood here for twenty years, watching the waves crash against the rocks, and I’ve never felt so alone.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since you told me you no longer wanted to be my daughter. I didn’t understand it then, and I still don’t. You were so young, so full of fire and fury, and I—well, I was just a man who had spent too long watching the sea and not enough time learning how to love you properly.

I’ve tried to forgive you, Eleanor. I’ve tried to forget the way your eyes turned cold when you said those words. I’ve tried to believe that time would mend what was broken. But it hasn’t. Time only makes the scars deeper, and the silence louder.

This storm is unlike any I’ve seen. The sky is black as ink, and the wind carries the scent of salt and sorrow. I’ve lit the lamp, as I always do, but I wonder if it’s enough. If it can outshine the darkness that creeps into my heart as surely as the waves creep into the harbor.

You were my light, Eleanor. I watched you grow

**[Sealed with a wax stamp bearing the crest of the Lighthouse of St. Agnes]**

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with a heart heavy with years of silence, and with the weight of a storm that threatens to break upon us all. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and though the sea may be vast, it cannot quench the ache of distance that has grown between us. I have longed for your return, but it seems the tides of time have carried you away, and I have been left to tend this lonely beacon upon the cliff.

Tonight, the heavens are dark with a storm that has gathered like a beast upon the horizon. The wind howls with a voice that sounds almost like a warning, and the sea, which has always been my companion, now roars with fury. I fear for my life, Eleanor. The lighthouse, though strong, is not impervious to the wrath of nature. I have seen storms before, but this one… it is unlike any I have known. I have lit the lamp, and I will keep it burning, as I always have, but I cannot shake the feeling that this may be the last night I see the dawn.

I have thought often of you in these years. I have imagined your face, your voice, your laughter—though I have not heard it in far too long. I have tried

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may not find you at all. I've spent the last five years in silence, not by choice, but by necessity. The sea has been my only companion, and the lighthouse my refuge. Yet tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the winds howl like the cries of the damned, I find myself yearning for your voice, your presence, your love.

You were my light in the darkest of times, and I have long since forgotten how to navigate without it. I have tried to fill the void with the monotony of duty, the rhythmic crashing of waves, and the flickering glow of the beacon. But the sea has a way of whispering secrets, and it has reminded me of the silence that has grown between us.

I have often wondered what became of you. Did you find your own path, or did you follow the one I once laid out for you? I remember the day you left, the tears in your eyes, the way you clung to your dreams as if they were the only thing that could save you from the life I had chosen. I was proud, yes, but also afraid—afraid that I would lose you to the world beyond this lonely shore.

Tonight, as the storm threatens to tear down the very walls of this lighthouse, I am reminded of how fragile life is. The wind has grown fierce, and the sea has

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the wails of the damned, and the sea roiling as though it seeks to swallow the very cliffs that have stood for generations. I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn, but if I do, I hope you will read this. If I do not, then perhaps this will be the last letter you ever receive from me.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of empty halls, of the lighthouse standing alone against the fury of the Atlantic. I have kept the light burning, as I always have, though I sometimes wonder if it is for the sea or for the ghosts of the past.

You were always so quick to leave, Eliza. I remember the day you stormed out, your face red with anger, your words sharp as the rocks below. You said I was cruel, that I never understood you. But I did understand you, child. I understood the pain you carried, the way you clung to your dreams like a child to a toy. You wanted more than this life, more than the cold stone walls and the endless nights. And I, in my stubbornness, could not give it to you.

But I did not mean to be cruel. I only wanted you to be safe. I wanted you to know the truth of this world, the weight of the sea, the silence that comes when the storm breaks.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you in good health, though I daresay it will find you in the company of your own thoughts, as I have found myself lately. Five years have passed since we last spoke, and the silence between us has grown like the sea—vast, unyielding, and full of secrets. I write to you now not with the hope of mending what has been broken, but with the earnest desire to share with you what has transpired in these long years, and to offer you a final farewell, should fate be so cruel as to claim me before this storm passes.

The wind howls tonight like a banshee, and the sea, that ever-faithful companion of mine, roars with a fury I have never known. The lighthouse, which has stood as a sentinel for so many years, now trembles under the weight of the storm. I have lit the lamp, and I shall keep it burning, for it is my duty, and perhaps, in this moment, it is also my prayer—that the light may guide those who sail these waters, even as it may not guide you back to me.

I have often thought of you in the quiet hours, when the world is still and the only sound is the ticking of the clock and the whisper of the sea. I remember the days when we would sit by the fire, and you would read to me, your voice as soft as the

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

**April 1st, 1887**

**Dear Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like the very devil himself outside this lighthouse, and the sea roiling as though it would swallow the very earth. I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn, but if I do, I hope to see you again—though I fear that even if I do, we may not be the same.

Five years have passed since you left, and in that time, the world has changed, as have I. The sea, once my only companion, now speaks to me in voices I cannot understand. The light that guides the ships has grown dimmer, as if the very stars have turned their backs on me. And the silence of this place, once comforting, now feels like a prison.

I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet I think of you every day. I wonder where you are, what you do, what you think of me. Do you remember the days when we would sit on the cliffs, watching the sun set over the horizon, and you would say, "Dad, when you grow old, will you still love me?" I told you then that love is not a thing that fades with time, but a light that never dies. I hope you still believe that.

I have tried to write to you, but the letters always seem

**April 12th, 1897**  
**St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isle of Wight**

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a banshee just beyond the stone walls of this old keep. The sea is restless tonight, and I fear the storm that brews on the horizon may be more than nature can conjure. I have kept this lighthouse for over twenty years, and never before have I felt the weight of the sea so keenly. I have always believed that the light must burn, no matter the cost—but tonight, I am not sure I will be here to see the dawn.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years since you walked out that door with your father’s name on your lips and your mother’s tears in your eyes. I have not heard from you, not a single letter, not even a postcard. I suppose it is not surprising. You were always the one who wanted more than this life—more than me. You wanted the city, the theatres, the company of people who could understand you. I was only a man who lived with the sea, who spoke to the wind, who could not offer you the world you craved.

But tonight, I have no one but the sea to speak to. The storm is gathering, and I fear the light may go out before the morning. I have already lit the lamp, but the

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. Five years have passed since the last time I saw you, and I fear the storm that now rages against this lighthouse may be the end of me. Yet, even as the wind howls and the waves crash against the rocks, I find myself thinking of you.

I have not spoken to you in so long, and I know I have no right to reach out after the way I left you. You were so young, and I was a fool to let pride and fear drive me away. I should have fought harder to keep you by my side, instead of pushing you away like the sea pushes the shore.

I have watched the skies all day, and the clouds grow darker with every passing moment. The lighthouse is my life, my duty, my anchor in a world that has drifted too far from me. But tonight, as the storm comes, I feel something stir within me—a longing, a regret, a wish that perhaps you might still listen.

If you are reading this, know that I am not the man I once was. I have aged, I have changed, and I have come to understand that silence is not the same as peace. I would give anything to have you here with me, to share the light and the dark, to speak once more as mother and daughter.

I pray the storm passes you by, and that you may find some peace in the

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy as the storm that brews beyond the cliffs. Five years have passed since the last time I laid eyes upon you, and yet the memory of your face haunts me more than the ghosts of the sea. I have not spoken to you, nor have you to me, and I fear the silence between us has grown deeper than the ocean itself.

You were a child when you left, and I, a fool, let you go. I told myself it was for your own good, that the world was too cruel, too cold for a girl of your tender years. But I was wrong, Eliza. The world is not cruel—it is indifferent. And I, a man who once believed himself strong enough to protect you, was but a shadow in the dark.

I have kept this lighthouse these many years, through tempests and tides, through the endless watching of the sea. It is my life, my duty, my curse. And now, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sky darkens with a fury I have not seen since the day you left, I find myself wondering—what if I do not survive this night?

You were always my light, Eliza. Even in the darkest hours, your presence brought me comfort. I would stand upon the tower, the salt wind in my face, and think of you, and feel a peace I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**  

I write this with the wind howling like a wounded beast outside the lighthouse, the sea roaring as though it would swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. The storm is coming, and I fear it may take more than the light from the tower.  

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, lonely years since you left. I have not heard from you, nor have I dared to write. The silence between us has grown heavier than the sea, and I have come to believe that perhaps it is not the silence that hurts most, but the weight of what lies beneath it.  

You were a child when you left, no older than ten. I remember the day as if it were yesterday—the storm, the rain, the way you clung to my coat as if it were the only thing keeping you from being swept away. You said you wanted to see the world, that you needed to find your own way. I did not stop you. I could not. Not then.  

But I have missed you, Eleanor. More than I ever dared to admit. I have watched the waves crash against the cliffs, I have lit the beacon each and every night, and I have wondered if you ever looked up at the light and thought of me. If you ever thought of home.  

This storm is unlike any I have seen. The sky is black, the wind is deafening, and the sea is rising. I fear for the lives

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**  

**From the cliffs of Culloden, on the eve of a storm that threatens to swallow the sea itself, I write this with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence.**  

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five years since I watched you walk away from this lighthouse, from me, from the life we once shared. I have not spoken your name aloud in all that time, not even to the wind or the sea. But tonight, as the sky darkens and the waves roar like beasts in pain, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in a decade.  

You were always the storm in my life, my Eleanor. A tempest of spirit, of passion, of questions that never ceased. You asked me things I could not answer, and I let you go because I feared I would not be the man you deserved. I told myself that you would find your way to a life more suited to your fire. But I never once stopped loving you.  

Tonight, as I stand at the edge of the cliff, the wind howling like a ghost, I wonder if you still remember the way the sea looked from this very spot. The way the waves would crash against the rocks, and the light would flicker in the tower like a dying star. I used to tell you that the lighthouse was more than a beacon—it was a promise. A promise that I would keep you

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence, for I find myself at the mercy of both the tempest and the weight of my own unspoken words. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, that ever-faithful companion, roars with a fury I have not heard since the day you left.

Five years have passed since you walked out of our home, and though I have not spoken your name in all that time, it lingers in my mind like the scent of the sea on a still night—familiar, yet distant. I have tended this lighthouse with unwavering devotion, as one might tend a dying flame, hoping against hope that it might yet burn bright once more. But tonight, the heavens themselves seem to conspire against me. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see dawn.

I do not write to beg for your forgiveness, for I have long since ceased to believe that I deserve it. Yet I cannot help but think of the nights I have stood upon this cliff, lantern in hand, watching the world below as it was once yours. You were a bright light in my life, Eleanor, and I have spent these years trying to keep the lamp alight, even as the darkness of my own guilt and sorrow threatened to extinguish it.

If you are reading this, know that I have not forgotten you. I have not forgotten

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a wounded beast outside this old tower, rattling the shutters and gnawing at the edges of my resolve. Five years have passed since you left, and yet the silence between us feels longer still. I have not spoken your name aloud in this house since the day you walked out, and now, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself questioning whether I will live to see the dawn.

You were always the clever one, Eleanor. The one who could see beyond the fog, beyond the sea, beyond the narrow world of this lighthouse. I used to admire that. I used to believe that if you could see the light, you would return. But you did not. You chose the city, the books, the life of a woman who could leave the sea behind. And I, I stayed.

I have always been the keeper of the light, but you were the keeper of my heart. You were the child who used to climb the tower with me, who would press her small hands against the glass and watch the waves, whispering secrets to the sea. I used to tell her stories of the drowned sailors and the ghost ships that drift through the mist. She would laugh and call them “the old men who never grew up.” I loved that.

But then you grew up. You left. And I, I stayed behind, clinging to the lamp and the rhythm

**[Seal of the Lighthouse, East Cliff, Isle of Wight]**  
**October 23rd, 1892**  

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**  

I write this with a hand that trembles, not from the cold, but from the weight of the words I must say. Five long years have passed since you left this house, and I have not spoken your name in all that time. Yet, as the wind howls and the sea roars with fury, I find myself compelled to reach out—to you, to the daughter I once held close, and to the daughter I have lost.  

The storm is upon us, Eleanor. The skies have darkened with a wrath I have not seen in many a year. The lighthouse stands, as it always has, a sentinel against the tempest, but I fear that even it may not hold. I have seen the sea rise, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that shakes the very foundations of this place. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, and though the light is steady, I cannot shake the feeling that this may be the last time I shall do so.  

You were always the light in my life, Eleanor. When I was young, you were my solace, my joy, my pride. And when I was old, you were the one who came to visit, and for a time, we spoke as mother

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear I may never receive a reply. Five years have passed since the last time our paths crossed, and though I have tried to forget, the weight of your absence has grown heavier with each passing season. I write to you now not with the hope of rekindling what was lost, but with the urgency of a man who knows his time is limited.

Tonight, the skies darken with a fury that has not been seen in decades. The wind howls like a banshee, and the sea, once a gentle companion, now roars with a vengeance. I can feel the very bones of this lighthouse tremble beneath me, and I am reminded that even the sturdiest of men can be undone by nature's wrath. I pray that the storm does not claim me before I can put pen to paper, for I have much to say and little time to say it.

I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper must be both a guardian and a guide, not only for the ships that pass by but for the souls that dwell within the heart of the sea. I have watched the sun rise and set for countless nights, each one a reminder of the fleeting nature of life. You, my dear Eleanor, were once the light that guided me through the darkest of nights, and I have missed your presence more than words can convey.

I know the world has changed since we last spoke.

My dearest Eliza,

I write this with trembling hands, the wind howling like a beast outside the lighthouse, and the sea, that ever-hungry thing, roiling with fury. I do not know if I shall live through this night, and if I do, I do not know if I shall ever see you again.

Five years have passed since you left. Five years of silence, of letters unopened, of nights spent staring at the horizon, wondering if you ever thought of me at all. I have not spoken to you, not truly, not since the day you told me you would not return. I told you then that I would not stop the light, that I would not let the sea claim us all. But I did not say that I would never see you again.

You were so young, Eliza. I remember the day you came to visit, all wrapped in your mother’s shawl, your eyes wide with wonder as you looked at the beacon, the great flame that never sleeps. You said you wanted to be a lighthouse keeper when you grew up. I told you that it was a lonely life, but you laughed and said you did not mind.

You did not understand, child, that loneliness is not a choice. That the sea is not kind. That even the strongest of men can be undone by the waves.

I have kept the light burning through every storm, every gale, every night of rain and wind. I

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I must confess, I write with a heavy heart. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and the silence between us has grown colder with each passing day. I have often thought of you, my dear Eleanor, and the days we shared, both joyful and sorrowful.

Tonight, the heavens have darkened with a storm that promises to be most fierce. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea, which has always been our steadfast companion, now roars with a fury that stirs both fear and awe within me. I have spent many an hour upon the cliffs, watching the waves crash against the rocks, and I have been reminded of the power of nature—both beautiful and merciless. I fear that the storm may not pass without cost, and I find myself wondering if I shall see the dawn.

I have often wondered if you have found peace in your own life, or if the weight of our past still burdens you. I know I have been absent, and I can only hope that my absence has not been a source of pain for you. I have tried, in my own way, to atone for the words and actions that may have caused you sorrow. I have come to understand the depth of my failings, and I am truly sorry for the pain I have caused you.

The lighthouse, which has been my home and my duty, has become a

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 3rd, 1892**  
**The Lighthouse, Farthing Island**

I write to you with trembling hands and a heart that feels as though it may never mend. It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I fear that time has not been kind to either of us. The sea, as ever, is a cruel and fickle mistress, and I find myself once more at her mercy—this time, perhaps, with no chance of escape.

Tonight, the sky has darkened with an ominous weight, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. The waves have grown restless, crashing against the rocks with a fury that shakes the very foundations of this old stone tower. I have lit the beacon, as I have done for many years, but I cannot shake the feeling that this may be the last time I shall do so.

You remember the stories I used to tell you as a child, of the sea's wrath and the courage of those who dared to stand against it. I hope you still remember them, for they are the only solace I have in this moment. I have always believed that the lighthouse is more than a structure of stone and glass—it is a promise, a beacon of hope for those who wander too far from the shore. And now, as I face this storm, I find myself wondering if I am not merely a sentinel of light, but also a man who

**April 12th, 1894**

**To My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you far from well. I have not written to you in five years, and I do not know if you will even read these words. But tonight, as the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars with a fury I have never known, I feel the need to speak, to be heard, even if it is only by the wind and the waves.

You were a child when I last saw you, standing in the doorway of our home, eyes wide with a mix of fear and wonder as I lit the lamp for the first time. I had promised you that I would always be there, that I would keep the light burning for you, for all those who sailed the dark waters. But I have not kept that promise. I have let the light go out, not in the sense of the lamp, but in the sense of my heart. I have let you go.

I know you think I abandoned you. That I chose the sea over your mother, over you. But I had no choice, Eliza. The lighthouse was my life, and I was its keeper. You were the one thing that made me feel whole. But I was not whole enough to be a father. I was not whole enough to be a husband. I was a man who had lost his way

My Dearest Eliza,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in the same place I have been—alone, with nothing but the sea and the wind to keep me company. It has been five years since we last spoke, and I have often wondered if you would ever write back, or if you have even remembered me. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the waves grow wilder, I felt the need to write, to say something, even if it is only this.

I have not been the same since you left. The lighthouse, once a place of solace, has become a prison. I used to think I could keep the light burning for you, but I have come to see that I have been keeping it for myself. The sea has always been my companion, but now it feels as if it is laughing at me. I do not know if I will survive this storm, and I do not know if I will survive the silence that has settled between us.

I have not forgotten you, Eliza. I have not stopped thinking of the day you walked out of our home, the way your eyes were distant, as if you were already gone. I have not stopped hoping that one day you might return, not as a child, but as the daughter I once knew. I would welcome you with open arms, even if you do not come.

If you read this, know that I am still here. I

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the ink of my own despair, for the wind howls like a beast at my door, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. The lantern burns low, and the light that guides the ships is but a flicker in the dark. I fear the night may claim me before dawn, and I have no one left to tell of the storm that took my breath—or of the silence that has taken my heart.

Five years have passed since you left, Eleanor. Five long, bitter years of watching the horizon for a sign that you might return. I have kept the lighthouse, as I have always kept it, but the light has grown dim in my soul, and the beacon that once shone with purpose now flickers like a candle in a gale.

You were my light, my daughter. I remember the day you first came to me, a child with eyes like the sea and a heart full of questions. You would sit by the lamp and ask me why the stars shone so bright, and I would tell you that they were the eyes of the heavens watching over us. You believed in that, Eleanor. You believed in the light.

But then you grew, and the world grew darker. You spoke of other things—of cities, of books, of lives that did not involve the salt of the sea or the call of the waves. You said I was too

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with the wind howling like a banshee in my ears and the sea roiling as though it might swallow the very rocks upon which I stand. The lantern is still burning—though I’ve not dared to leave the tower for hours. I’ve been watching the sky, waiting for the storm to break, and now, at last, it is coming. I pray the heavens have mercy on us all.

Five long years, Eleanor. Five years since you left this house, this life, and this father. I have not spoken to you, not even in the silence of the night, for fear that if I did, I might say something I could not take back. But tonight, the weight of it all is too much. I cannot bear the silence any longer.

I know you believe I abandoned you. That I turned my back on you when the world was still young and the path ahead seemed uncertain. But I was not the one who chose to walk away. I was the one who stayed, who kept the light burning, who held the tower together, who waited for you to come back. And you did not. You never did.

I have seen the way the villagers look at me now, with their pity and their judgment. They say I am a man who has lost everything. But I have not lost everything, Eleanor. I still have the sea, the wind, the sky—and the duty that binds me to this

**To My Dearest Elara,**

**April 12th, 1893**  
**Lighthouse of St. Agnes, Isle of Wight**

I write to you in the flickering light of the beacon, the wind howling like a wounded beast at the windows. The storm is coming, and I fear it may not be the sea that takes me, but the weight of silence between us.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have spent them watching the waves, counting the hours, and waiting for a letter that never came. I have not spoken to you, not even in the way a father might to a daughter, and yet I have never stopped thinking of you. You are the only thing that keeps me from turning off the lamp and walking away from this lonely post.

I know you do not want to hear from me. I know you have your own life, your own family, your own world. I have no right to intrude. But tonight, as the sky darkens and the sea roars like a beast, I feel the need to speak. Perhaps it is the storm that has driven me to it, or perhaps it is the memory of your face, still so clear in my mind, that has made me write.

I do not know if you will read this. I do not know if you will believe it. But I must say it: I am sorry. I am sorry for the way I treated

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you this evening, as the sky darkens and the wind howls like a beast unleashed. The sea, which has always been my companion, now roars with a fury I have not known since the day I first took up the lamp and the duties of this lonely post. The storm is upon us, and I fear the worst. If the heavens should break upon this rock, I may not live to see the dawn.

It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I have not forgotten the last words we exchanged. You called me a fool, and I, in my pride, did not answer. I have since pondered that, and I must say, I do not believe you were wrong. I have been a man of habit, of duty, of silence. I have let the light burn without the warmth of your voice, and I have let the sea wash away the words I should have spoken.

I have always believed that a lighthouse keeper must be steadfast, that he must stand firm against the elements, and that he must never waver. But I now wonder if there is a time when even the most steadfast must bend. I have seen the world from these cliffs, Eleanor, and I have watched the ships pass by, some safe, some lost. I have seen the storms come and go, and I have seen the sun rise again after the darkest nights. Perhaps there is a lesson in that, and perhaps

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with a trembling hand, the wind howling like a vengeful spirit through the cracks in the lighthouse walls. The sea is restless tonight, and I fear it may not be the storm alone that takes me. But I must say these words before the night swallows me whole, before the silence between us grows too deep to ever be bridged.

Five years, Eleanor. Five long, cruel years. I have watched the waves crash against the rocks, and I have watched the silence between us grow just as relentless. You left, and I did not stop you. I thought, perhaps, that it was for the best. That you would find a life beyond this place, beyond the cold, the loneliness, the weight of duty. But I was wrong.
